title: Operational comfort
principles: Operational comfort summarizes the effort, preparation, positioning, tracking, and interruption involved in using an attack mode.
Every comfort conclusion must be traceable to an explicit and previously validated friction record.
Handling, preparation, interruption, positioning, and tracking friction must remain separate because they describe different operational demands.
Friction frequency, severity, conditions, and whether the cost is mandatory must be evaluated independently.
An operational advantage may reduce the practical weight of a directly related friction but must not erase or offset unrelated limitations.
A mixed profile should be preserved when comfort differs materially across operational dimensions.
Operational comfort must remain separate from damage potential, statistical strength, combat function, and subjective enjoyment.

comfortable: The evaluated mode has little mandatory or recurring operational friction across the supplied dimensions.
Any supported costs are limited, infrequent, or narrowly conditional.
manageable: The mode has noticeable operational friction, but no supplied friction source consistently dominates its normal operation.
The specific sources and conditions must remain stated.
demanding: At least one supported friction source is substantial, frequent, or mandatory during normal operation.
The explanation must identify the dominant operational demand.
mixed: The mode is comfortable in some operational dimensions and demanding in others.
Preserve the dimensional profile instead of reducing it to an unqualified average.
undetermined: The supplied friction records are absent, incomplete, or insufficiently validated for a reliable comfort classification.

handling_friction_present: Preserve accuracy, spread, recoil, stability, and contact-control evidence as distinct handling causes when supplied.
Do not treat one handling statistic as proof of another.
preparation_friction_present: Treat charge, wind-up, spool-up, or another required preparation stage as preparation friction only when its timing and activation role are supplied.
Do not merge ranged charge timing with melee heavy-attack wind-up.
interruption_friction_present: Use a validated reload, recharge, recovery, or staged-interruption profile.
Do not infer interruption severity from reload time or magazine size alone.
positioning_friction_present: Treat limited reach as positioning friction only when the attack mode requires the user to operate within that supplied reach.
Do not reinterpret limited reach as poor accuracy, recoil, or tracking.
tracking_friction_present: Use projectile travel, target acquisition, beam contact, or movement-sensitive evidence only when their practical tracking effect is explicitly supported.
Do not classify projectile or beam delivery itself as demanding.
operational_advantage_present: Apply the advantage only to the friction dimension it directly affects.
Do not let a long firing window erase handling, tracking, preparation, or positioning friction.
multiple_modes_present: Classify operational comfort separately for each relevant mode.
Do not average incompatible mode profiles into one whole-weapon rating without an explicit relevance criterion.

exceptions: Do not infer aiming difficulty from accuracy, recoil, projectile delivery, beam range, or melee range without validated practical evidence.
Do not infer demanding operation from high fire rate, attack speed, charge labels, or projectile classification alone.
Do not let favorable damage, critical, status, multi-target behavior, or special mechanics improve the comfort classification.
Do not treat conditional friction as permanently active.
Do not merge friction evidence across attack modes with different operational behavior.
